summary: Verified issue #4's 1.4.1 checklist item verbatim via `gh issue view 4`: acceptance criteria, test spec name (TestContentCreate, -run TestContentCreate -race), and impacted modules (engine/catalog/content.go, engine/catalog/content_test.go) match the implementer's claim exactly. Read content.go in full: ContentStore.Create builds wal.NewCatalogPutRecord(rec.FileID, encoded) and delegates to the real, already-verified engine/wal.AppendAndApply (not a stub/no-op) -- confirmed wal.Writer.Append fsyncs the record to disk before returning (writer.go doc comment + code), and AppendAndApply only invokes the apply callback (content file write + cat.Put) after Writer.Append returns successfully, so WAL-before-apply is a structural guarantee enforced by the wal package's control flow, not an accident of code ordering. writeContentFile uses temp-file+rename for crash-safe atomic content writes. ContentPath produces exactly content/<fileID>.v1.md via filepath.Join + fmt.Sprintf("%d%s", fileID, ".v1.md"); fileID is uint64 so no path-traversal/string-injection surface exists. content_test.go's TestContentCreate is a genuine, non-tautological ordering proof: an internal afterWALBeforeApply hook independently re-reads the WAL segment from disk via wal.ReadSegment/DecodeTypedRecord/AsCatalogPut (not shared state with the Writer) to confirm the catalog-Put record is durable, and simultaneously asserts os.Stat on the content path fails with ErrNotExist and cat.Get returns ErrNotFound at that exact point -- i.e. it actually observes the pre-apply state, not just checks a final outcome. Post-Create it asserts exact byte-equality of on-disk content vs input and reflect.DeepEqual of the committed catalog record. TestContentCreateInvalidFileID is a real guard test (Create with InvalidFileID errors, verified via table-free direct call). Ran go build ./... && go vet ./... (both clean) and go test ./catalog/... -race -v -count=1 (all pre-existing catalog tests plus both new content tests pass, 56s) and full engine/... -race -count=1 (btree, catalog, wal all green) -- zero regressions. Diff is purely additive: no changes to engine/wal or any pre-existing engine/catalog file.

requirements.evidence: Acceptance criteria and test spec from `gh issue view 4` match implementer's stated design and the actual code/tests verbatim. content/<fileID>.v1.md path shape, WAL-logged-before-committed ordering, and content-byte fidelity are all independently confirmed by direct code trace and by running the exact test-spec command.

architecture_conformance.evidence: Consumes engine/wal's already-verified public API (wal.NewCatalogPutRecord, wal.AppendAndApply) exactly as issue #3's WAL layer was designed to be used, matching docs/LLD/wal.md's stated invariant ('every mutation to the catalog or any index must be logged in the WAL before it is applied'). However both docs/LLD/catalog.md and docs/LLD/wal.md remain 'scaffold only' placeholders (last_synced_commit predates this and several prior subtasks) and were not updated by this diff to document ContentStore's existence or contract -- this is a continuation of the LLD-staleness gap flagged in prior verification runs (e.g. 042-verification), not a new defect introduced here.

regression_risk.evidence: Diff is purely additive: two new files only, no changes to engine/wal or any existing engine/catalog file. Full engine/... -race -count=1 suite (btree, catalog, wal) passes with zero regressions, including all pre-existing catalog tests (catalog_test.go, file_test.go, idalloc_test.go, page_test.go, record_test.go).

edge_cases.evidence: InvalidFileID guard is tested. Path-traversal via fileID is structurally impossible (uint64 type, %d formatting, no string ever taken from the fileID field). Not tested: (1) calling Create twice for the same fileID -- Catalog.Put is an upsert (tombstones the old slot) and writeContentFile's temp+rename will silently overwrite the existing content/<fileID>.v1.md, so a duplicate/overwrite Create silently succeeds with no idempotency or already-exists guard; this may be intentional for Phase 1 but is undocumented and untested. (2) empty-content and very-large-content inputs are not exercised by any test, though writeContentFile's write path has no special-casing that would plausibly break on either. Neither is required by the acceptance criteria/test spec, so flagged as coverage gaps rather than blocking defects.

security.evidence: fileID is a uint64, never a caller-supplied string, so ContentPath cannot be used for path traversal or directory escape regardless of catalog-record content. No other new external input surface introduced.

maintainability.evidence: Code is well-commented, correctly cross-references the wal.AppendAndApply contract and engine/wal/record_test.go's TestFsyncBeforeApply idiom it mirrors, and cleanly separates ContentStore from Catalog per the LLD's stated module boundaries. contentVersionSuffix hardcoding 'v1' is explicitly and clearly flagged as a deliberate pre-MVCC scope limit with a pointer to where multi-version naming should land later.

test_coverage.evidence: TestContentCreate genuinely exercises the required WAL-before-catalog-visibility ordering via an independent-of-Writer-state observation (fresh os.ReadFile + decode of the WAL segment, plus os.Stat/cat.Get checks) at the exact pre-apply instant, not a tautological or end-state-only assertion. TestContentCreateInvalidFileID is a real guard test. Matches the literal test-spec command (go test ./engine/catalog/... -run TestContentCreate -race). Gaps: no test for duplicate-fileID Create (overwrite) semantics, no empty/large-content test, no test for a WAL-append-succeeds-but-apply-fails partial-state scenario (acknowledged in code comments as deferred, consistent with Phase 1 scope, but worth a follow-up test alongside a later recovery-integration subtask).

explicit_determinations.wal_before_apply_is_structural: CONFIRMED: wal.Writer.Append fsyncs the record to the WAL segment file before returning (writer.go, confirmed by direct code read of file.Sync() call preceding the return), and wal.AppendAndApply only invokes its apply callback after Append returns successfully. content.go's createWithHook passes content-write + cat.Put as that apply callback. This is enforced by the wal package's own control flow (the caller cannot invoke apply before Append returns), not by code-ordering convention alone.
explicit_determinations.content_path_shape: CONFIRMED: ContentPath(fileID) = filepath.Join(dir, fmt.Sprintf("%d.v1.md", fileID)) where dir = filepath.Join(root, "content") -- produces content/<fileID>.v1.md exactly as specified.
explicit_determinations.byte_fidelity: CONFIRMED: writeContentFile writes data via tmp.Write(data) with no transformation, encoding, or newline normalization; TestContentCreate asserts exact string equality of on-disk bytes vs the input slice.
explicit_determinations.path_traversal: NOT POSSIBLE: fileID is uint64; the only string interpolation into the path is via %d, so no attacker-controlled string content ever reaches filepath.Join.
explicit_determinations.partial_apply_failure_handling: Code comments and wal.AppendAndApply's documented contract correctly describe apply-failure-after-durable-append as a 'requires later replay/recovery' scenario rather than a rollback scenario, consistent with WAL semantics, but this specific interaction (content-write-fails-after-WAL-append) has no dedicated test in this subtask; acceptable for Phase 1 scope per the requirement's own framing, but should not be forgotten by whichever later subtask wires WAL replay to ContentStore.

commands_run: grep engine/wal/record.go for NewCatalogPutRecord, AppendAndApply; read AppendAndApply body and doc comment
cd engine && go build ./... && go vet ./...
go test ./... -race -count=1 (full engine module, all green)

test_run_summary.full_catalog_package: go test ./catalog/... -race -v -count=1: pass, 13 top-level tests / subtests, 0 failures, ~56s
test_run_summary.full_engine_module: go test ./... -race -count=1: pass across engine/btree, engine/catalog, engine/wal; no test files in engine/graph, engine/loadtest, engine/mvcc, engine/rpc, engine/split
